text:
I see little chance (probably zero) of a US refiner buying Iranian oil over next 60 days. So why did the White House made such an emphatic move to waive sanctions on US purchases of Iranian oil?

a) Gesture to Tehran with little consequence?
b) Painful Iranian imposition?

quotedTweet.text:
It may only last 60 days (I doubt it; likely would become permanent), but the US Treasury’s waiver is effectively rolling back 40-year plus of American oil sanctions against Iran. 

Even US refiners are allowed to import Iranian oil and pay for it in greenback!!! https://t.co/nG7xDt1Fwj

text: US put ~9.1 million barrels of SPR oil into the market last week, the 2nd largest weekly release ever (equal to ~1.3 million b/d). Over the last 8 weeks, the US has been releasing >1m b/d on average.

⛽️US / Japan / Europe SPR barrels still flowing 
🇮🇷  Iranian oil now legal for (at least) next 60 days
🇨🇳China remains on an oil buying strike

It may only last 60 days (I doubt it; likely would become permanent), but the US Treasury’s waiver is effectively rolling back 40-year plus of American oil sanctions against Iran. 

Even US refiners are allowed to import Iranian oil and pay for it in greenback!!! https://t.co/nG7xDt1Fwj

BREAKING: US Treasury issues a general license waiving key Iran oil sanctions until August 21, 2026. On a quick read, it’s a rather wide ranging license, covering exports, shipping and ancillary services, and includes crude, refined products and petrochemicals. https://t.co/N4ayfQzg4C

Crucially, the general license not only allows importers of Iranian oil to pay Tehran (including via previously sanctioned entities), **but also do so in U.S. dollars!**

For 60 days at least, Washington is allowing Tehran back in the dollar club to repatriate its petrodollars.

Iran/US/mediators set up a mechanism for keeping SoH open
Iran has agreed to invite back nuclear inspectors
".... the agreements reached in this session [...] will facilitate the process of implementing reciprocal commitments..."

quotedTweet.text:
هیأت نمایندگی جمهوری اسلامی ایران بعد از انجام گفتگوهای فشرده درباره اجرای مفاد یادداشت تفاهم خاتمه جنگ مورخ ۲۸ خرداد ۱۴۰۵، عازم میهن است.

این گفتگوها با تمرکز بر بندهای ۱، ۵، ۱۰ و ۱۱ متن یادداشت تفاهم، از صبح یکشنبه ۳۱ خرداد در سوئیس (Lake Luzern) شروع شد و تا ساعات اولیه بامداد دوشنبه ۱ تیر ادامه یافت. بر اساس بیانیه مشترک کشورهای میانجی (قطر و پاکستان) که با مشورت ایران و آمریکا تنظیم شد، ساز و کارهای اجرایی برای نظارت بر اجرای مفاد یادداشت تفاهم پیش‌بینی شد و مقرر گردید گفتگوها در سطوح کارشناسی و فنی برای پیشبرد اجرای موثر تفاهم خاتمه جنگ تحمیلی ادامه یابد.

با توجه به اینکه طبق بند ۱۳ یادداشت تفاهم، آغاز مذاکرات برای توافق نهایی، منوط به شروع و تداوم اجرای بندهای ۱، ۴، ۵، ۱۰ و ۱۱ است، توافق‌های صورت گرفته در این نشست (به‌ویژه بند ۱ در رابطه با خاتمه جنگ و عملیات نظامی رژیم صهیونیستی در لبنان از طریق ایجاد یک سازوکار کنترل منازعه با مشارکت طرفین و جمهوری لبنان، و نیز بندهای ۱۰ در رابطه با صادرات نفت و محصولات پتروشیمی ایران و ۱۱ موضوع آزادسازی دارائی‌های مسدودشده ایران) تسهیل‌کننده روند اجرای تعهدات متقابل خواهد بود.

 مبنای کار، «تعهد در مقابل تعهد» است و جمهوری اسلامی ایران ضمن رصد اجرای تعهدات طرف مقابل، از همه اهرم‌های خود برای اطمینان از اجرای آن تعهدات بهره خواهد گرفت.

China's oil imports keep falling and falling in June, below the 10-year low set in May.

According to @Vortexa, June 1-20 imports of crude and condy are at 5.3m b/d, down>50% from pre-war levels. 

I don't see this as sustainable and a recovery looms (helped by Iranian barrels).

The Iranian delegation is sounding upbeat, saying that “oil and petrochem exports are waived.” U.S. Treasury / OFAC hasn’t yet published any new guidance. Iran has been exporting lots of crude in recent days, and it’s loading oil tankers again at Kharg Island.

U.S. geothermal advocates press for revival

Geothermal electricity generation remains very small-scale but it enjoys support across the political spectrum and is well matched to meet fast-growing demand from data centres so there is significant potential for growth.

Policy support could be sustained through multiple electoral cycles, future changes in administration and congressional control, if geothermal can maintain support from both major parties and both fossil and non-fossil industries.

There is potential for rapid growth from a low base as part of an all-the-above approach to meeting the rising demand for electricity ...

CHINA’s crude oil processing slowed to 54 million tonnes in May, the slowest for nearly four years, when the country was still in the grip of travel restrictions and lockdowns to control the coronavirus. Imports slowed even faster to just 33 million tonnes from 38 million in April and 50 million in March in response to the disruption of flows through the Strait of Hormuz. As a result, roughly 2 million tonnes (15 million barrels) of crude appear to have been pulled from inventories:

text:
Another important caveat to these exit flows.

While loadings have indeed begun to rise, led above all by the UAE (master of the shadow transits), those loadings are still running at less than half the pace of these boosted exits.

Inherently unsustainable without more ships in.

quotedTweet.text:
One of the bigger changes with this latest rush of exiting Hormuz transits is that we're seeing more VLCCs* in the mix, which is bumping up the total exit volume.

By my count, 10-day rolling exits hit a new Hormuz crisis high pace of ~8.5 million barrels per day over the weekend.

Exit pace still notably outstripping pace of newly entering tankers, which are the ones we'll need to restart production and sustain this exit pace much longer as we draw down long-stranded stranded floating stock.

* Very Large Crude Carrier carrying ~2 million barrels

Speaking on the tarmac before he left Switzerland Monday, Vice President JD Vance said he only trusts actions amid negotiations with Iran.

"You can't trust anybody's words. You have to trust what they actually do," he told reporters.

Earlier, the vice president said that Iran would allow nuclear inspectors to return to the country.

"Letting in the inspectors is a big deal," he said on the tarmac. "But again, we're going to see what they actually let the inspectors do once they're in the country. That's going to continually be a part of our negotiation."

"So my point is not that I trust or distrust anybody," he continued. "My point is that I trust actions and what the president has asked us to do is verify what they're doing, focus less on what they're saying." https://t.co/PHUAaYFYdj

Vice President Vance said he did not feel snubbed by Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi when he appeared to skip out on a photo op with him on Sunday.

"Trust me, I've spent a lot of time dealing with the Iranians over the last few months. Sometimes I find them extremely confusing as negotiators," he told reporters Monday following hours of meetings with Iranian negotiators and mediators in Switzerland.

Vance said there was a "sort of social media firestorm" suggesting the Iranians were going to leave the talks.

"And then we proceeded to talk to them for like the next nine hours. So I would just encourage the media, mistrust a little bit what you see coming out of Iranian social media. They can be confusing negotiators, but we feel like we're making progress," he said. https://t.co/FVbBJ91e7M

The US Department of Interior is slashing the bonds oil and natural gas companies must provide when drilling on federal land by 95% in a move to encourage more energy exploration.

The bond required in onshore federal drilling leases will be cut to $25,000 from the $500,000 imposed during the Joe Biden administration, the department announced Monday. Such bonds ensure that the costs of capping and cleaning up any wells abandoned by drillers don’t fall to taxpayers.

COT data on #FX covering the week ending 16 June showed continued demand for the USD, albeit at a much slower pace following the recent surge in bullish positioning. Against eight IMM currency futures, the aggregate USD net long rose 6% to USD 29.6 billion, the highest level in 14 months. Renewed demand for the EUR helped offset selling across several other currencies, most notably the AUD, NZD, and CAD.
The JPY short hit a fresh two-year high at 150k contracts, the equivalent of USD 11.8 billion.

COT on #commodities covering managed money positioning in week to 16 June.
Another week of broad selling led by Brent, soybeans, corn, sugar and cotton, while some demand emerged in precious metals, in gold driven by short covering during the slump to near USD 4,000 while the silver long rose 23%.

The grains sector has seen a dramatic positioning reset over the past six weeks. During that period, the BCOM Grains Index fell 7.5%, while the combined net long across the six major contracts tracked collapsed by 90% from a record 874k contracts. Corn has borne the brunt of the liquidation, with speculators swinging from a net long of 344k contracts to a net short of 46k contracts in the latest reporting week.

#Commodities

Brent crude trades back below USD 79 a barrel after briefly rising to USD 82.30 following a weekend of US-Iran peace talks. Despite a bumpy start, with Trump issuing fresh warnings towards Iran and Tehran responding with renewed threats to close the Strait of Hormuz, negotiations nevertheless showed signs of progress. The market continues to price in the prospect of an eventual reopening of the Strait and the release of millions of barrels currently stranded in the Persian Gulf. That expectation helped drive a sharp increase in bearish positioning, with hedge funds raising gross Brent short positions to a pandemic-era high in the week to 16 June.

Gold trades higher near USD 4,200, supported by lower oil prices and a softer dollar after a three-day decline that followed last week's hawkish FOMC meeting. For now, the yellow metal remains stuck in technical limbo, trading between key support at USD 4,000-4,100 and resistance from the 200-day moving average, currently near USD 4,466.
